blocks.statement: I can capture a v–t graph for a falling ball with a Vernier motion detector and find g from the slope.

blocks.whatWeKnow: We've been USING g = 9.81 m/s² for a week. Today we MEASURE it.
blocks.connection: If our lab number matches theory, the F = G·m₁·m₂/r² framework we'll apply to 2026-XJ stands on tested ground.

blocks.title: Investigation 2.1 — Measure g
blocks.markdown:
**Driving question:** How close can we come to measuring g = 9.81 m/s² in this room?

**Equipment:** Vernier motion detector + LabQuest (1 per pair) · Logger Pro or Graphical Analysis on a laptop · a drop ball (≥ 4 cm diameter — bigger is better for the sensor) · tape measure (to set drop height) · clear floor zone (head and toes out of the drop path).

**How this lab serves the year's question:** if the lab confirms g near Earth, the equations we use for 2026-XJ are trustworthy.

blocks.title: What you do
steps: Point the motion detector UP. Hold the ball at a known height above it.
Start data collection. Release the ball cleanly (no toss).
On the v–t graph, the falling portion is a straight line. Fit it. **Slope = g.**
Repeat for 3 trials. Average. Compare to 9.81 m/s².

blocks.title: Safety
blocks.markdown: Catch the ball **before** it hits the sensor. Keep hands and faces clear of the drop path. Don't release toward another group.

blocks.title: Not in class today?
blocks.markdown: Run the picket-fence simulation below — it's the same measurement: an object falls past a sensor, you read g from the slope of the v–t graph. Use its values to fill your data table.

blocks.instruction: Sketch your lab setup: detector pointing up, ball above it, drop height h marked. Record your h value on the diagram.
prompts: Show the detector, the ball, and the drop path.
Label the drop height h with its value.
Mark the 'clear zone' around the drop path.

blocks.patternPrompt: Row 5 is your average. How consistent are your slopes across trials?
How close is your AVERAGE to 9.81 m/s²? Compute your percent error.
blocks.interpretPrompt: If your value is LOW, what in your setup might cause that? If HIGH, what might? And the asteroid connection: how does this measurement serve the year's question?
blocks.frame: My measured g was ___ m/s². The accepted value is 9.81 m/s². My percent error was ___. I think the biggest source of error was ___.

blocks.prompt: Write a claim-evidence-reasoning paragraph about your measurement.
blocks.frame: From my data, I claim g = ___ m/s². My evidence is ___. This is ___% off from 9.81 m/s². I think the difference is because ___.
